elements.text: may also be of interest here:

An increasing number of researchers see their work as interactive authoring tools or software substrates for interactive computational media. By talking about “authoring tools”, we remove the divide between programmers and users; “software substrates” let us look beyond conventional programming languages and systems; and “interactive computational media” promises a more malleable and adaptable notion of tools for thought we are striving for. This workshop aims to bring together a wide range of perspectives on these matters.

Researchers in education, design, and software systems alike have long explored how computation can become visible and adaptable to its users, from diSessa’s learner-centered Boxer microworlds of the 80s, Hypercard, and Maclean et al’s Buttons. Even mundane systems such as spreadsheets blur the distinction between use and creation.

The notion of a substrate is an umbrella for many different traditions which are bringing local agency over software systems.

[...many more words, see the site!...]

We welcome participation from workers from academia, industry, independent scholars, and others, from any of the communities named above, and from any others who can see their goals reflected in the substrate agenda.
The submission deadline for this workshop is 2026-02-20.

text: Thanks for posting that. I quite enjoyed it. It strengthened my conclusion that creativeness is being crushed out of existence by our insistence on building programming languages and workflows aimed solely at the waterfall-esque production-engineering of machine code. Just about every example in the video was built in assembler or some non-strict programming language. Inventiveness requires iteration and the ability to quickly change your mind and to throw away all code produced up to that point.

text:
Just a small shout-out. I keep coming back to <@UBN9AFS0N>'s <https://instadeq.com/blog/categories/cat_nocodehistory/|No-code History> blog posts. They're such a great way to start learning about (or page back in) some of the most interesting, historically significant projects in our field. Each has a nice summary of the project and historical context, a bunch of quotes and videos and extracts, and then canonical resources for digging deeper. Wonderful stuff.

• <https://instadeq.com/blog/posts/no-code-history-pygmalion-1975/|Pygmalion>
• <https://instadeq.com/blog/posts/no-code-history-the-geometry-theorem-machine-1959/|Geometry Theorem Machine>
• <https://instadeq.com/blog/posts/no-code-history-graphical-input-language-grail-1969/|GRAIL>
• <https://instadeq.com/blog/posts/no-code-history-frox-a-scriptable-smarttv-with-a-magic-wand-1991/|Frox>
• <https://instadeq.com/blog/posts/no-code-history-sketchpad-a-man-machine-graphical-communication-system-1963/|Sketchpad>
• <https://instadeq.com/blog/posts/no-code-history-peridot-uis-by-example-visual-programming-constraints-1987/|Peridot>
• <https://instadeq.com/blog/posts/no-code-history-lotus-improv-spreadsheets-done-right-1991/|Lotus Improv>
So — thank you, Mariano!

blog posts. They're such a great way to start learning about (or page back in) some of the most interesting, historically significant projects in our field. text:
I've been doing some deeper research about Sketchpad lately, and only just discovered that the Computer History Museum has a wonderful online collection. You can create an account, and then browse through their archives adding items to your own little personal collections. For whatever reason, this "it's bookmarks, but just for this one website" feels really nice to me?

What sort of stuff will you find on the CHM website?
• <https://www.computerhistory.org/collections/catalog/search-catalog/search/keyword:tx-2-module/|200 photos of modules from the TX-2>, for some reason!!
• A <https://www.computerhistory.org/collections/catalog/102682003/search/keyword:sutherland/|coffee mug> branded <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evans_%26_Sutherland|Evans & Sutherland>, who pioneered head-mounted displays and did a bunch of early work in 3d graphics
• Almost 500 <https://www.computerhistory.org/collections/catalog/search-catalog/search/collection:oral-histories--format:mov-57-mp4-61/|oral> history videos (why did I only put the hyperlink on "oral"? am I having a laugh?)
• The source code for <https://computerhistory.org/blog/adobe-photoshop-source-code/?key=adobe-photoshop-source-code|Photoshop version 1.01> — don't sleep on the _delightful_ photos of the Knoll brothers at the top of this post. Someone should put these on a tee shirt.
• A video of ma<https://www.computerhistory.org/collections/highlights/page/2/|g>netic RAM (okay, now you're just being daft on purpose).
For some reason, I find the CHM youtube channel irritating, but _love_ browsing their website. And that reason is design. The website feels like the website of a computer history museum. That's a nice feeling. (Can you imagine if the Exploratorium had a website that felt like the Exploratorium? Why the hell haven't they done that?)

text: Last one for now — <http://www.bitsavers.org|Bitsavers> has an incredible archive of software, photos, PDFs, magazines, and other historical materials. A real goldmine if you're looking to place yourself, say, back at MIT in '63.

text: I found the <https://youtu.be/qS20Z0RXr28?si=Es3DQZaYCa0Z-Ror&t=3060|point> where Alan alluded that the Parc GUI "won" because it was more intersectional (art, theatre, etc.) research interesting. I never heard any historical recounts of that, and it doesn't gauge well with my understanding of why Parc's GUI became ubiquitous. Anyone have any insights?

elements.text: where Alan alluded that the Parc GUI "won" because it was more intersectional (art, theatre, etc.) research interesting. I never heard any historical recounts of that, and it doesn't gauge well with my understanding of why Parc's GUI became ubiquitous. Anyone have any insights?

text:
Until Jobs commercialized some of what he saw at Parc, the only interface to commercial "home" computers was the MSDOS and CP/M command line. UNIX was essentially only available to students at universities, and it only had a command line interface. Even the Apple II was command-line based. You could create graphics, but as single apps, not GUIs that acted as the main HCI. I think that the Lisa was the first GUI-based home computer. Perplexity says:
> First commercial GUI: Xerox Star (1981, office workstation). ~$16,500.00 ($55,000.00 in today's money)
> First successful consumer home GUI: Macintosh (1984, $2,495).
> Lisa bridged the gap but flopped commercially (~100K units sold, ~$10,000.00).
Apple coupled the Mac with a Postscript printer and made the desktop publishing market accessible and affordable for "home" use.

Further, Perplexity says:
Timeline Context
• Macintosh (Jan 1984): Hardware GUI debut.
• LaserWriter/PostScript (Mar 1985): Enables DTP.
• Windows 1.0 (Nov 1985): Microsoft’s first GUI OS, tiled windows, mouse support; initial sales ~500K.
• X11, the first stable version of the X Window System protocol, appeared on September 15, 1987.
So, I would agree that the Parc GUI won out, at least in terms of the home-affordable computer. Apple targeted the "creative" markets, starting with better looking fonts and postscript, branching out to DAWs (buying out Emagic (Logic)). Microsoft targeted the "office" market. Apple carved out the "creative" market, IBM tried to push OS/2 and eventually gave up.

UNIX was essentially only available to students at universities, and it only had a command line interface. Even the Apple II was command-line based. You could create graphics, but as single apps, not GUIs that acted as the main HCI. I think that the Lisa was the first GUI-based home computer. Perplexity says:

First commercial GUI: Xerox Star (1981, office workstation). ~$16,500.00 ($55,000.00 in today's money)
First successful consumer home GUI: Macintosh (1984, $2,495).
Lisa bridged the gap but flopped commercially (~100K units sold, ~$10,000.00).

Apple coupled the Mac with a Postscript printer and made the desktop publishing market accessible and affordable for "home" use.

Further, Perplexity says:
Timeline Context
Windows 1.0 (Nov 1985): Microsoft’s first GUI OS, tiled windows, mouse support; initial sales ~500K.
X11, the first stable version of the X Window System protocol, appeared on September 15, 1987.

So, I would agree that the Parc GUI won out, at least in terms of the home-affordable computer. Apple targeted the "creative" markets, starting with better looking fonts and postscript, branching out to DAWs (buying out Emagic (Logic)). Microsoft targeted the "office" market. Apple carved out the "creative" market, IBM tried to push OS/2 and eventually gave up.
